title: GitLab Security Essentials - Hands-On Lab: DAST and API Scans
headings: GitLab Security Essentials - Hands-On Lab: DAST and API Scans
Objectives
Task A. Setting up DAST Scans
Task B. Setting up API Scanners
Lab Guide Complete
text:
GitLab Security Essentials - Hands-On Lab: DAST and API Scans

Estimated time to complete: 40 minutes

Estimated time to complete: 40 minutes

Many projects are deployed as web applications with API components. To be able to scan these components, you can utilize the DAST and API security scanners.

In this lab, you will learn how to implement both scanners for your projects.

Task A. Setting up DAST Scans

To test out DAST scans, we are going to set up an instance of a vulnerability web application called OWASP Juice Shop . Scanning this application will show you the full range of DAST scan results you can expect to see.

Create a new blank project. Name the project DAST .

Create a new blank project. Name the project DAST .

In the DAST project, create a .gitlab-ci.yml file.

In the DAST project, create a .gitlab-ci.yml file.

To start, add the DAST stage to your configuration: stages : - dast

To start, add the DAST stage to your configuration:

DAST currently uses a CI/CD template, which we will include just below our stages. include : - template : DAST.gitlab-ci.yml

DAST currently uses a CI/CD template, which we will include just below our stages.

Since we don’t have a dedicated server, we will opt to pass the Juice Box application into DAST as a Docker service. To do this, start by defining the service below the template include: dast : services : - name : bkimminich/juice-shop:v16.0.0 alias : juiceshop

title: GitLab Security Essentials - Hands-On Lab: DAST and API Scans

text:
start by defining the service below the template include: dast : services : - name : bkimminich/juice-shop:v16.0.0 alias : juiceshop

Since we don’t have a dedicated server, we will opt to pass the Juice Box application into DAST as a Docker service. To do this, start by defining the service below the template include:

We can provide many different variables to our DAST scanner. We will add the following values to the DAST scanner: variables : DAST_TARGET_URL : "http://juiceshop:3000/" DAST_AUTH_URL : "http://juiceshop:3000/#/login" DAST_FULL_SCAN : "false" DAST_AUTH_USERNAME : "admin@juice-sh.op" DAST_AUTH_PASSWORD : "admin123" # use protected/masked variables, this is only for demonstration purposes DAST_AUTH_USERNAME_FIELD : "css:input[id=email]" DAST_AUTH_PASSWORD_FIELD : "css:input[id=password]" DAST_AUTH_SUBMIT_FIELD : "css:button[id=loginButton]" DAST_SCOPE_EXCLUDE_ELEMENTS : "css:[id=navbarLogoutButton]" DAST_AUTH_REPORT : "false" DAST_REQUEST_COOKIES : "welcomebanner_status:dismiss,cookieconsent_status:dismiss" DAST_CRAWL_GRAPH : "false" To highlight some variables, DAST_TARGET_URL and DAST_AUTH_URL provide a target for scanning and authentication. We provide credentials for authentication with DAST_AUTH_USERNAME and DAST_AUTH_PASSWORD . The DAST_AUTH_USERNAME_FIELD and related field variables tell DAST where to input login data. Our remaining settings are scan settings to help reduce the scan time for this demonstration.

We can provide many different variables to our DAST scanner. We will add the following values to the DAST scanner:

The DAST_AUTH_USERNAME_FIELD and related field variables tell DAST where to input login data. Our remaining settings are scan settings to help reduce the scan time for this demonstration.

After entering all of these values, your yaml file should look like this: stages : # List of stages for jobs, and their order of execution - dast include : - template : DAST.gitlab-ci.yml dast : services : - name : bkimminich/juice-shop:v16.0.0 alias : juiceshop variables : DAST_TARGET_URL : "http://juiceshop:3000/" DAST_AUTH_URL : "http://juiceshop:3000/#/login" DAST_FULL_SCAN : "false" DAST_AUTH_USERNAME : "admin@juice-sh.op" DAST_AUTH_PASSWORD : "admin123" # use protected/masked variables, this is only for demonstration purposes DAST_AUTH_USERNAME_FIELD : "css:input[id=email]" DAST_AUTH_PASSWORD_FIELD : "css:input[id=password]" DAST_AUTH_SUBMIT_FIELD : "css:button[id=loginButton]" DAST_SCOPE_EXCLUDE_ELEMENTS : "css:[id=navbarLogoutButton]" DAST_AUTH_REPORT : "false" DAST_REQUEST_COOKIES : "welcomebanner_status:dismiss,cookieconsent_status:dismiss" DAST_CRAWL_GRAPH : "false"

After entering all of these values, your yaml file should look like this:

Commit these changes and let the DAST scan run. You can monitor the job progress from Build > Pipelines . Note: This job can take up to 15 minutes to complete.

Task B. Setting up API Scanners

API scanners allow you to scan your application API endpoints for potential vulnerabilities. To demonstrate this process, we will use an application template which contains an API configuration.

Navigate to your Security Labs project.

Navigate to your Security Labs project.

Take some time here to review the postman_collection.json file. This file contains the definitions required to run API scanning against the application in this project. After reviewing the file and structure, you can proceed with enabling API scanning.

Take some time here to review the postman_collection.json file. This file contains the definitions required to run API scanning against the application in this project. After reviewing the file and structure, you can proceed with enabling API scanning.

Navigate to Code > Repository and edit .gitlab-ci.yml .

text:
scanning.

Take some time here to review the postman_collection.json file. This file contains the definitions required to run API scanning against the application in this project. After reviewing the file and structure, you can proceed with enabling API scanning.

Navigate to Code > Repository and edit .gitlab-ci.yml .

Navigate to Code > Repository and edit .gitlab-ci.yml .

To add API scanning to our container, define the dast stage and add the API security template. include : #Other scanners would be here as well. - template : API-Security.gitlab-ci.yml stages : - build - test - dast

To add API scanning to our container, define the dast stage and add the API security template.

Add the job definition for the API scanner. api_security : services : - name : $CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE/$CI_COMMIT_REF_SLUG:$CI_COMMIT_SHA alias : target variables : APISEC_POSTMAN_COLLECTION : postman_collection.json APISEC_TARGET_URL : http://target:7777

Add the job definition for the API scanner.

Commit these changes and view the results once the pipeline completes.

Commit these changes and view the results once the pipeline completes.
